 =20
   =20
      What are the 3AC guys up to?
   =20
 =20

I have   a model of Adam Neumann that goes like this. There was a bubble fo=
r a particular sort of tech startup, one that scaled=C2=A0very quickly and =
used lots of buzzwords and had big ambitions and bad unit economics and rai=
sed tons of money from SoftBank Group Corp. Adam Neumann saw that bubble an=
d said =E2=80=9Cwell, I should be on the other side of that.=E2=80=9D The o=
ther side of that bubble was=C2=A0not=C2=A0shorting a bunch of tech startup=
 stocks: That=E2=80=99s risky, and anyway you couldn=E2=80=99t really do it=
 because the bubble was in tech=C2=A0startups=C2=A0with no publicly traded =
stock.
No, the other side of the bubble was=C2=A0starting a startup, and making it=
 the most egregious imaginable example of the bubble. Scale the fastest, ta=
lk the most nonsense, have the worst unit economics and raise the most mone=
y by bro=E2=80=99ing down the most with=C2=A0SoftBank=E2=80=99s Masayoshi S=
on. Neumann incinerated many billions of dollars of SoftBank=E2=80=99s mone=
y, and got paid something like a billion dollars to stop. What a great trad=
e! He and his family will be wealthy for generations because he timed the s=
tartup bubble right, but also because he structured the trade right. The tr=
ade is not quite =E2=80=9Cgo long startups=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cgo short st=
artups=E2=80=9D; the trade is =E2=80=9Cother people want to get long this b=
ubble, so stand in the way of their money.=E2=80=9D
I should say that I do not think that this model is=C2=A0accurate, in the s=
ense that it does not correctly describe what Adam Neumann subjectively tho=
ught he was doing. I find it useful, though, because it does=C2=A0describe=
=C2=A0what he was doing. Why is Adam Neumann rich? Because SoftBank made a =
mistake that involved spending $10 billion to build a $400 million company,=
 and Adam Neumann was there to take the other side of that mistake.
If you spotted a gigantic crypto bubble in the late 2010s and early 2020s, =
how would you play it? Two obvious wrong answers:
=09Short crypto. You=E2=80=99d have gotten carried out, multiple times.=09L=
ong crypto. This is better =E2=80=94=C2=A0the George Soros=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWh=
en I see a bubble forming, I rush in to buy=E2=80=9D   approach =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0but still risky=C2=A0(the bubble did pop) and sort of analytically un=
satisfying.

And a pretty good answer:
=09Take out absolutely bajillions of dollars of non-recourse loans to buy a=
s much crypto as you can, selling enough along the way =E2=80=94=C2=A0and p=
utting the proceeds somewhere your creditors can=E2=80=99t get them =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0to make yourself dynastically wealthy.
  [1]
=C2=A0Borrow $1 billion to buy $1 billion worth of crypto. If that turns in=
to $2 billion of crypto, pay off your loans, take the extra $1 billion and =
bury it in your backyard, and do it again. If you do it again and it turns =
into $0 of crypto, walk away from your debts, dig up your backyard and buy =
yachts.

The Adam Neumanns of the crypto bubble might have been Kyle Davies and Su Z=
hu, the founders of Three Arrows Capital. Three Arrows, or 3AC as everyone =
calls it, is usually=C2=A0called a =E2=80=9Ccrypto hedge fund,=E2=80=9D but=
 that name is not really=C2=A0accurate. Typically a hedge fund raises money=
 from investors, invests it, gives the profits to investors and takes a cut=
. But 3AC does not seem to have invested much money for outside investors.
Instead 3AC invested using its partners=E2=80=99 capital and immense oceans=
 of=C2=A0leverage=C2=A0from crypto lending platforms. This leverage was pro=
vided with very little in the way of due diligence or negotiation or often =
even collateral. And 3AC was very clear-eyed and thoughtful about identifyi=
ng=C2=A0this=C2=A0part of the bubble. The bubble was not just =E2=80=9Cpeop=
le buy crypto and it goes up=E2=80=9D; it was specifically =E2=80=9Cpeople =
invest a lot of money in crypto lending platforms, which promise a high rat=
e of interest but have nowhere good to lend their money, so we will just bo=
rrow=C2=A0all their money from them, pay them interest, and use the money t=
o make=C2=A0insanely risky crypto bets. If they pay off, we buy yachts; if =
they go bust, we don=E2=80=99t pay them back and sail away on our yachts.=
=E2=80=9D This worked for a while and made the 3AC guys rich, and then it s=
topped working and made 3AC=E2=80=99s=C2=A0lenders bankrupt. But the 3AC gu=
ys took money off the table along the way, and they will be wealthy for the=
 rest of their lives. A bunch of crypto lending platforms had too much mone=
y and wanted to do something dumb with it. 3AC provided the dumb thing to d=
o with it, and took a large fee for that service.
I actually think that this=C2=A0might=C2=A0be what Davies and Zhu subjectiv=
ely thought they were doing? (It is not what they said; Zhu was famously a =
proponent of the idea of a crypto =E2=80=9Csupercycle=E2=80=9D in which, li=
ke, crypto assets could only go up forever.) We   talked last December abou=
t  an interview Kyle Davies gave to Hugh Hendry, which I think is the most =
lucid explanation of the=C2=A0crypto credit bubble that you will find anywh=
ere. Davies does not seem particularly=C2=A0naive. There is a certain glee =
with which he describes how desperate the lending platforms were to give hi=
m money, and how happy he was to take it. Why is Kyle Davies rich? Well,   =
Celsius and Voyager and Genesis=C2=A0made=C2=A0billions of dollars of mista=
kes, and he was on the other side of those mistakes.
Anyway this weekend David Yaffe-Bellany at the New York Times  checked in o=
n Davies and Zhu and they are very, very happy with their life choices:

One clear evening, on a rooftop in Bali, Mr. Davies took shrooms with a gro=
up of crypto colleagues. =E2=80=9CYou look at the stars, and the stars are =
just, like, moving,=E2=80=9D he recalled over dinner last month at a seafoo=
d restaurant in Barcelona, Spain, where he was vacationing with his wife an=
d two young daughters. =E2=80=9CYou touch the grass, and it feels, like, no=
t like normal grass.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=A6
Mr. Davies and Mr. Zhu maintain that they did nothing wrong. They said they=
 had faced death threats, but pointed out that no government agency had sue=
d them or sought their arrests.
A friend recently asked Mr. Davies whether he felt any remorse. =E2=80=9CRe=
morse for what?=E2=80=9D he said he had replied. =E2=80=A6
Mr. Zhu said his lawyers had assured him that Three Arrows=E2=80=99 actions=
 were =E2=80=9Cwhiter than white.=E2=80=9D By the time the firm was liquida=
ted last June, he and Mr. Davies were in Bali. Mr. Zhu was learning to surf=
. Mr. Davies bought a paint set and started experimenting with still lifes.
=E2=80=9CYou eat very fatty pork dishes, and you drink a lot of alcohol, an=
d you go to the beach and you just meditate,=E2=80=9D Mr. Davies said as he=
 recounted his travels. =E2=80=9CYou have these magical experiences.=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=A6
Mr. Davies said he was ready to move on from Three Arrows by the end of las=
t summer. =E2=80=9CI really spent so much time meditating in Bali that I=E2=
=80=99m really just pretty zenned out,=E2=80=9D he said.

Yes right=C2=A0he=C2=A0is zenned out. He played the crypto bubble perfectly=
.

   =20
      UBS bought Credit Suisse
   =20
 =20

Sometimes a financial services firm will take too many bad risks, and it wi=
ll go bust. And the normal way to go bust, for a financial services firm, i=
s that the failed firm will be=C2=A0bought, generally for a small amount of=
=C2=A0money, by some other, bigger firm that did not take as many risks. A =
big relatively sedate firm buys the firm that flew a bit too close to the s=
un. But the failed firm doesn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0disappear; its assets and busi=
nesses and employees are still there, and the sedate firm buys them, and th=
en it decides what to do with them. And conceptually there are two approach=
es:
=09=E2=80=9CThey took too much risk, which led them to go bust. We are smar=
ter than that, so we are going to ruthlessly impose=C2=A0our=C2=A0safer and=
 more sedate culture on them, and get rid of all the dumb risks that led th=
em to go bust.=E2=80=9D=09=E2=80=9CWe always admired their risk-taking and =
were kind of bored with ourselves, and now we were able to buy them cheap. =
Oh oh oh we are not going to take=C2=A0as many risks as they did =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0that blew them up after all =E2=80=94=C2=A0but surely we can carefull=
y graft=C2=A0some=C2=A0of their risk-taking culture onto our sedate culture=
 in a way that will still be safe enough, but let us have more fun than we=
=E2=80=99ve been having.=E2=80=9D

I   like to   say=C2=A0around   here that one of the best career moves in f=
inance is to lose a billion dollars: Sure you=E2=80=99ll get fired, but you=
=E2=80=99ll easily get a new job, because potential employers will be impre=
ssed by the amount of money that was trusted to you and your confidence in =
taking risks with it. There might be a collective version of this: If a who=
le firm has=C2=A0an aggressive risk-taking culture, that might=C2=A0bankrup=
t=C2=A0it, sure, but potential buyers will be intrigued. Bear Stearns   had=
 a big influence on JPMorgan=E2=80=99s trading business. The  most prestigi=
ous   alumni network in finance might be  Drexel=E2=80=99s. Drexel=E2=80=99=
s culture worked out poorly for Drexel, but lots of other firms wanted it.
That=E2=80=99s not always how it goes though. UBS Group AG  closed its acqu=
isition of Credit Suisse Group AG today, and UBS is at least=C2=A0saying=C2=
=A0that it doesn=E2=80=99t want any of Credit Suisse=E2=80=99s old attitude=
.   Bloomberg News reports:

UBS Group AG said a slew of top Credit Suisse Group AG executives will leav=
e, while others will take on lesser roles, as the larger bank exerts its do=
minance following the historic takeover.=C2=A0
High-profile departures include Credit Suisse Chief Financial Officer Dixit=
 Joshi and co-head of the investment bank David Miller. Only a fifth of the=
 160 leadership positions in the combined bank announced Monday are coming =
from Credit Suisse, according to a UBS spokeswoman. =E2=80=A6
UBS executives including Chairman Colm Kelleher have made clear that the re=
maining Credit Suisse bankers would be put through a =E2=80=9Cculture filte=
r=E2=80=9D to weed out undesirable practices from the defunct bank.=C2=A0

And the Financial Times adds that  yacht loans are out:

UBS has imposed tight restrictions on Credit Suisse bankers including a ban=
 on new clients from high-risk countries and on complex financial products =
after completing the takeover of its ailing rival on Monday.
UBS executives have drawn up a list of nearly two dozen =E2=80=9Cred lines=
=E2=80=9D that prohibit Credit Suisse staff from a range of activities from=
 the first day the two banks are combined, according to people with knowled=
ge of the measures.
Prohibited activities include taking on clients from countries such as Liby=
a, Russia, Sudan and Venezuela and launching new products without approval =
from UBS managers. ...
=E2=80=9CWe are worried about =E2=80=98cultural contamination=E2=80=99,=E2=
=80=9D UBS chair Colm Kelleher said last month over taking on Credit Suisse=
 staff. =E2=80=9CWe are going to have an incredibly high bar for who we bri=
ng into UBS.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
UBS executives fear they are taking on a bank that has traditionally been m=
uch more willing to accept risky clients and offer them high-stakes product=
s. Credit Suisse=E2=80=99s final few years as an independent company were m=
arked by a series of scandals and crises, which one internal report said we=
re a result of its =E2=80=9Clackadaisical attitude towards risk=E2=80=9D. .=
..
Under the rules, Credit Suisse bankers are unable to trade in a range of ar=
cane financial products, including Korean derivatives and options of certai=
n quantitative indices. ...
Credit Suisse employees must also ask UBS executives for permission to exte=
nd loans backed by assets such as yachts, ships and real estate of more tha=
n $60mn.

Some banks, in some markets, might be really excited by the rare opportunit=
y to get into the oligarch yacht loan business, but UBS wants to avoid cult=
ural contamination.

 =20
   =20
      Stablecoins
   =20
 =20

Are stablecoins securities? A typical stablecoin =E2=80=94=C2=A0Tether, Cir=
cle=E2=80=99s USDC, Binance=E2=80=99s BUSD, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0works like =
this:
=09You give $1 to a company; it gives you back a crypto token (the stableco=
in).=09The company presumably puts the $1 somewhere safe =E2=80=94=C2=A0a b=
ank account, Treasury bills, something like that =E2=80=94=C2=A0though ther=
e is certainly room for hijinks here, and your legal rights are not necessa=
rily=C2=A0great.=09The company promises to give you back the $1 on demand.=
=09The company presumably earns interest on its investments, which makes th=
is a nice business, for it.=09The company mostly does=C2=A0not=C2=A0pay you=
 interest on your stablecoin.

This is a little loose, but accurate enough for our purposes.
  [2]
=C2=A0Does that make it a security? The US Securities and Exchange Commissi=
on  says, eh, sort of:

Using prongs of the so-called Howey test=E2=80=94a court precedent that giv=
es conditions under which something can be considered a security=E2=80=94th=
e SEC alleged in its Binance complaint that BUSD buyers participated in a c=
ommon enterprise with Binance, which did =E2=80=9Cearn returns through vari=
ous forms of capital deployment.=E2=80=9D It also said that Binance markete=
d BUSD=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cprofit-earning potential,=E2=80=9D including a re=
ward program that the SEC said =E2=80=9Cpromised interest payments to BUSD =
investors merely for holding BUSD.=E2=80=9D
Notably, the SEC said an expectation of profit for BUSD investors derived f=
rom these rewards, which it described as =E2=80=9Cthe potential for direct,=
 interestlike payments made by Binance, in part from the proceeds of deploy=
ing BUSD investors=E2=80=99 capital.=E2=80=9D
This may be a key argument for the SEC, since it must confront the contenti=
on that stablecoins aren=E2=80=99t expected by buyers to appreciate in valu=
e but instead to stand-in for a dollar, euro or other traditional currency =
and intended to be used for things like payments or storing value.

Ehh? Mainly, when we   talk about whether cryptocurrencies are securities, =
  what we are talking about is the Howey test. US  securities law says that=
 various things, including any =E2=80=9Cnote, stock, ... bond, debenture, e=
vidence of indebtedness, certificate of interest or participation in any pr=
ofit-sharing agreement, collateral-trust certificate, preorganization certi=
ficate or subscription, transferable share [or]=C2=A0investment contract,=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0are securities. =E2=80=9CInvestment contract=E2=80=9D=C2=A0i=
s the vaguest of those terms, so when the SEC argues that something weird a=
nd novel is a security, it usually argues that it=E2=80=99s an investment c=
ontract. And the Howey test says that an investment contract is =E2=80=9Can=
 investment of money in a common enterprise with profits to come solely fro=
m the efforts of others.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
But a stablecoin is obviously not that, because it does not promise profits=
. (Sometimes crypto exchanges offer an=C2=A0interest-bearing account where=
=C2=A0the exchange=C2=A0will pay interest on your stablecoin accounts, and =
the SEC   has argued that=C2=A0those=C2=A0accounts are investment contracts=
, but that doesn=E2=80=99t make the stablecoin itself a security.)
My view is that a stablecoin is probably not a security and the SEC is wron=
g about BUSD, but I don=E2=80=99t hold that view strongly. I do think, howe=
ver, that if BUSD=C2=A0is=C2=A0a security, it=E2=80=99s not because it=E2=
=80=99s an =E2=80=9Cinvestment contract.=E2=80=9D Look at the list of secur=
ities.=C2=A0Is a stablecoin =E2=80=9Cevidence of indebtedness,=E2=80=9D or =
a =E2=80=9Cnote=E2=80=9D? I mean, kind of? The stablecoin issuer=E2=80=99s =
promise to redeem the stablecoin for $1 is=C2=A0not quite the same sort of =
legally binding promise you=E2=80=99d find in a bond debenture, but at the =
same time stablecoin investors=C2=A0obviously=C2=A0expect the thing to be r=
edeemable at least in some indirect way; if investors didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0t=
reat=C2=A0the stablecoin as debt then it wouldn=E2=80=99t work. And =E2=80=
=9Cevidence of indebtedness,=E2=80=9D marketed broadly to retail investors,=
 is a security.
  [3]

The other item on the list that intrigues me is: =E2=80=9Cstock.=E2=80=9D
  [4]
 A close analogy for a stablecoin is that it is   a share of a money-market=
 mutual fund, and a share of a money-market fund is a security, because it =
is technically a share of stock. I   suggested last week that the best argu=
ment for crypto assets being securities is not to tick=C2=A0through the pro=
ngs of the Howey test to prove that they are =E2=80=9Cinvestment contracts,=
=E2=80=9D but to step back a bit and say =E2=80=9Cthese assets are obviousl=
y a way for crypto projects to raise money by issuing a close analogue of=
=C2=A0stock that is not technically called stock, and so they should be reg=
ulated as stocks.=E2=80=9D And similarly stablecoins are a close analogue o=
f money-market fund shares, so perhaps they should be regulated as money-ma=
rket fund shares.=C2=A0
Incidentally the item that is=C2=A0not=C2=A0on the list that intrigues me i=
s: A stablecoin is largely a=C2=A0bank account, and a stablecoin issuer is =
largely a=C2=A0bank. To the extent that a stablecoin is issued by a regulat=
ed bank or trust company (and some are), it should not be a security, becau=
se it is a bank deposit and   bank deposits are not securities =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0because they are subject to (generally much stricter!) bank regulation. =
To the extent that a stablecoin is just issued by some unregulated guys, it=
 =E2=80=A6=C2=A0also should not be a security, but bank regulators should h=
ave some concerns?

 =20
   =20
      Axie Infinity
   =20
 =20

If I told you that  US venture capitalists=C2=A0promoted=C2=A0a   Ponzi sch=
eme that used=C2=A0a   cartoon computer game to steal hundreds of millions =
of dollars from  poor workers in the Philippines and send it to=C2=A0North =
Korea to fund a=C2=A0ballistic missile program, you probably wouldn=E2=80=
=99t believe me. Unless I said =E2=80=9C=E2=80=A6=C2=A0using crypto,=E2=80=
=9D in which case you would probably say =E2=80=9Coh yeah that sounds about=
 right.=E2=80=9D The Wall Street Journal  reported this weekend:

Last year an engineer working for the blockchain gaming company Sky Mavis t=
hought he was on the cusp of a new job that would pay more money.
A recruiter had reached out to him via LinkedIn, and after the two spoke ov=
er the phone, the recruiter gave the engineer a document to review as part =
of the interview process.
But the recruiter was part of a vast North Korean operation aimed at bringi=
ng in funds to the cash-poor dictatorship. And the document was a Trojan Ho=
rse, malicious computer code that gave the North Koreans access to the engi=
neer=E2=80=99s computer and allowed hackers to break into Sky Mavis. Ultima=
tely they stole more than $600 million=E2=80=94mostly from players of Sky M=
avis=E2=80=99s digital pets game, Axie Infinity.=C2=A0
It was the country=E2=80=99s biggest haul in five years of digital heists t=
hat have netted more than $3 billion for the North Koreans, according to th=
e blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis.=C2=A0That money is being used to f=
und about 50% of North Korea=E2=80=99s ballistic missile program, U.S. offi=
cials say, which has been developed in tandem with its nuclear weapons.

Super! Venture capitalists have largely pivoted from crypto to artificial i=
ntelligence, and while the popular view is that AI has a=C2=A0higher=C2=A0p=
robability of wiping out humanity than crypto does, =E2=80=9Ccrypto funds=
=C2=A0the North Korean missile program=E2=80=9D=C2=A0would=C2=A0be a funny =
way for crypto to kill us all before a rogue AI can.

 =20
   =20
      Jeff Bezos bought one Amazon share
   =20
 =20

Sure I dunno   whatever:

Jeff Bezos, the world=E2=80=99s third-richest person, did something strange=
 two weeks ago: He bought a single share of Amazon.com Inc. for $114.77.=C2=
=A0
The share purchase, disclosed in a filing, is his first in records going ba=
ck to 2002. The Amazon founder is better known for selling =E2=80=94 he=E2=
=80=99s unloaded roughly $30 billion worth of stock since 2002 to fund his =
various pursuits, from rocket company Blue Origin to personal pleasures lik=
e his new $500 million superyacht Koru. ...
The May 25 stock purchase has made him about $10 richer, as Amazon shares t=
raded around $124 on Friday, boosted by a broader tech rally that pushed th=
e S&P 500 Index into a bull market this week.
Bezos=E2=80=99 move has confounded Amazon analysts and stock watchers who d=
on=E2=80=99t know what to make of the singular purchase. Social media was a=
buzz with theories, with one person speculating that Bezos planned to give =
the physical certificate as a gift and others quipping that he must have ac=
cidentally clicked the buy button on his brokerage account. Amazon and a re=
presentative for Bezos declined to comment on the purchase.

If I were Jeff Bezos I=C2=A0would absolutely open a Robinhood account and b=
uy one share of Amazon and then email my disclosure lawyers the confirmatio=
n saying =E2=80=9Clol sorry.=E2=80=9D If I=C2=A0bought one share of Amazon,=
 nobody would write articles about it. But when Jeff Bezos did, people did.=
 Is that not reason enough? Two big problems in modern economics are turnin=
g attention into money, and turning money into attention; buying one share =
of Amazon is a pretty efficient way to do the latter.

  [1] Incidentally this is in theory a possible explanation of   what Bill =
Hwang was up to, though I have never seen any evidence that he actually too=
k money off the table. But he certainly did take out bajillions of dollars =
of non-recourse loans to load up on risky stocks.


  [2] In particular I am being casual about the redemption method: Not ever=
y stablecoin promises to cash every owner out for $1, for operational and k=
now-your-customer and other reasons. The mechanism can be more like =E2=80=
=9Cthe stablecoin issuer has a few big counterparties, crypto exchanges and=
 big hedge funds and the like, whom it will cash out for $1 per stablecoin,=
 and the big counterparties will buy stablecoins for about a dollar because=
 they can cash out=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 sort of like authorized participants =
in the exchange-traded-fund world. This perhaps undermines the case for =E2=
=80=9Cevidence of indebtedness=E2=80=9D that I make below.


  [3] In fact there is   a whole separate body of caselaw distinguishing th=
e sorts of debt that are securities from the sorts that aren=E2=80=99t secu=
rities, and I am not *sure* that the SEC would win on this line of argument=
, but it seems easier than the Howey argument.


  [4] Or I guess "collateral-trust certificate," or "transferable share."

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong> <em>Money S=
tuff was supposed to be off today but I got bored so here you go.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">What are the 3AC guys up to?</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/31767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlua=
W9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMTAtMjMvaG93LWRvLXlvdS1saWtlLXdlLW5vdz9jbXBpZD1CQkQw=
NjEyMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ=
1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA2MTImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a2=
3cBc4711e1c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blan=
k" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom=
-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; back=
ground-color: #ccc;">  a model of Adam Neumann</a> that goes like this. The=
re was a bubble for a particular sort of tech startup, one that scaled=C2=
=A0very quickly and used lots of buzzwords and had big ambitions and bad un=
it economics and raised tons of money from SoftBank Group Corp. Adam Neuman=
n saw that bubble and said =E2=80=9Cwell, I should be on the other side of =
that.=E2=80=9D The other side of that bubble was=C2=A0<em>not</em>=C2=A0sho=
rting a bunch of tech startup stocks: That=E2=80=99s risky, and anyway you =
couldn=E2=80=99t really do it because the bubble was in tech=C2=A0startups<=
em>=C2=A0</em>with no publicly traded stock.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No, the other side of the bubble was=C2=A0<em>=
starting a startup</em>, and making it the most egregious imaginable exampl=
e of the bubble. Scale the fastest, talk the most nonsense, have the worst =
unit economics and raise the most money by bro=E2=80=99ing down the most wi=
th=C2=A0SoftBank=E2=80=99s Masayoshi Son. Neumann incinerated many billions=
 of dollars of SoftBank=E2=80=99s money, and got paid something like a bill=
ion dollars to stop. What a great trade! He and his family will be wealthy =
for generations because he timed the startup bubble right, but also because=
 he structured the trade right. The trade is not quite =E2=80=9Cgo long sta=
rtups=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cgo short startups=E2=80=9D; the trade is =E2=80=
=9Cother people want to get long this bubble, so stand in the way of their =
money.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I should say that I do not think that this mod=
el is<em>=C2=A0</em>accurate, in the sense that it does not correctly descr=
ibe what Adam Neumann subjectively thought he was doing. I find it useful, =
though, because it <em>does=C2=A0</em>describe=C2=A0what he<em> was</em> do=
ing. Why is Adam Neumann rich? Because SoftBank made a mistake that involve=
d spending $10 billion to build a $400 million company, and Adam Neumann wa=
s there to take the other side of that mistake.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you spotted a gigantic crypto bubble in the=
 late 2010s and early 2020s, how would you play it? Two obvious wrong answe=
rs:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Short crypto. You=E2=80=99d have gotte=
n carried out, multiple times.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Lon=
g crypto. This is better =E2=80=94=C2=A0the George Soros=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWhen=
 I see a bubble forming, I rush in to buy=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZX=
JnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDYtMDIvYW1jLWJyaW5ncy1vdXQtdGhlLXBvc=
GNvcm4_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDYxMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291=
cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNjEyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7ddf15da" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  approach</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0but =
still risky=C2=A0(the bubble did pop) and sort of analytically unsatisfying=
.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And a pretty good answer:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Take out absolutely bajillions of doll=
ars of non-recourse loans to buy as much crypto as you can, selling enough =
along the way =E2=80=94=C2=A0and putting the proceeds somewhere your credit=
ors can=E2=80=99t get them =E2=80=94=C2=A0to make yourself dynastically wea=
lthy.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: =
transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Borrow $1 billion to buy $1 billion worth of crypto. If that turn=
s into $2 billion of crypto, pay off your loans, take the extra $1 billion =
and bury it in your backyard, and do it again. If you do it again and it tu=
rns into $0 of crypto, walk away from your debts, dig up your backyard and =
buy yachts.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Adam Neumanns of the crypto bubble might h=
ave been Kyle Davies and Su Zhu, the founders of Three Arrows Capital. Thre=
e Arrows, or 3AC as everyone calls it, is usually=C2=A0called a =E2=80=9Ccr=
ypto hedge fund,=E2=80=9D but that name is not really=C2=A0accurate. Typica=
lly a hedge fund raises money from investors, invests it, gives the profits=
 to investors and takes a cut. But 3AC does not seem to have invested much =
money for outside investors.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instead 3AC invested using its partners=E2=80=
=99 capital and immense oceans of=C2=A0<em>leverage=C2=A0</em>from crypto l=
ending platforms. This leverage was provided with very little in the way of=
 due diligence or negotiation or often even collateral. And 3AC was very cl=
ear-eyed and thoughtful about identifying=C2=A0<em>this=C2=A0</em>part of t=
he bubble. The bubble was not just =E2=80=9Cpeople buy crypto and it goes u=
p=E2=80=9D; it was specifically =E2=80=9Cpeople invest a lot of money in cr=
ypto lending platforms, which promise a high rate of interest but have nowh=
ere good to lend their money, so we will just borrow=C2=A0all their money f=
rom them, pay them interest, and use the money to make=C2=A0insanely risky =
crypto bets. If they pay off, we buy yachts; if they go bust, we don=E2=80=
=99t pay them back and sail away on our yachts.=E2=80=9D This worked for a =
while and made the 3AC guys rich, and then it stopped working and made 3AC=
=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>lenders </em>bankrupt. But the 3AC guys took money off =
the table along the way, and they will be wealthy for the rest of their liv=
es. A bunch of crypto lending platforms had too much money and wanted to do=
 something dumb with it. 3AC provided the dumb thing to do with it, and too=
k a large fee for that service.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I actually think that this=C2=A0<em>might=C2=
=A0</em>be what Davies and Zhu subjectively thought they were doing? (It is=
 not what they <em>said</em>; Zhu was famously a proponent of the idea of a=
 crypto =E2=80=9Csupercycle=E2=80=9D in which, like, crypto assets could on=
ly go up forever.) We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=
ick/31767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGV=
zLzIwMjItMTItMDUvY3J5cHRvLWhhZC1hLWNyZWRpdC1idWJibGU_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDYxMjIzX0=
1PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlc=
m09MjMwNjEyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9ae986=
f6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">  talked last December</a> about <a href=3D"https://link.mail.=
bloombergbusiness.com/click/31767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cueW91dHViZS5jb20=
vd2F0Y2g_dj1Uekdka0IweGJDRSZ0PTFz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1c26ef25" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;=
"> an interview</a> Kyle Davies gave to Hugh Hendry, which I think is the m=
ost lucid explanation of the=C2=A0crypto credit bubble that you will find a=
nywhere. Davies does not seem particularly=C2=A0<em>naive</em>. There is a =
certain glee with which he describes how desperate the lending platforms we=
re to give him money, and how happy he was to take it. Why is Kyle Davies r=
ich? Well, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3176746=
6.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDctMT=
gvdGhyZWUtYXJyb3dzLXNhaWQtdG8tb3dlLWRpZ2l0YWwtY3VycmVuY3ktZ3JvdXAtMS0yLWJpb=
Gxpb24_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDYxMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291=
cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNjEyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8d5c1d57" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  Celsius and Voyager and Genesis<=
/a>=C2=A0made=C2=A0billions of dollars of mistakes, and he was on the other=
 side of those mistakes.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway this weekend David Yaffe-Bellany at the=
 New York Times <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31=
767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyMy8wNi8wOS90ZWNobm9sb2d5L=
3RocmVlLWFycm93cy1jcnl0by1iYWxpLmh0bWw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe4de87c2" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> checked in on Davies and Zhu</a> and they are very, very happy with=
 their life choices:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One clear evening, on a rooftop in Bali, Mr. D=
avies took shrooms with a group of crypto colleagues. =E2=80=9CYou look at =
the stars, and the stars are just, like, moving,=E2=80=9D he recalled over =
dinner last month at a seafood restaurant in Barcelona, Spain, where he was=
 vacationing with his wife and two young daughters. =E2=80=9CYou touch the =
grass, and it feels, like, not like normal grass.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=A6</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Davies and Mr. Zhu maintain that they did =
nothing wrong. They said they had faced death threats, but pointed out that=
 no government agency had sued them or sought their arrests.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A friend recently asked Mr. Davies whether he =
felt any remorse. =E2=80=9CRemorse for what?=E2=80=9D he said he had replie=
d. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Zhu said his lawyers had assured him that =
Three Arrows=E2=80=99 actions were =E2=80=9Cwhiter than white.=E2=80=9D By =
the time the firm was liquidated last June, he and Mr. Davies were in Bali.=
 Mr. Zhu was learning to surf. Mr. Davies bought a paint set and started ex=
perimenting with still lifes.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CYou eat very fatty pork dishes, and y=
ou drink a lot of alcohol, and you go to the beach and you just meditate,=
=E2=80=9D Mr. Davies said as he recounted his travels. =E2=80=9CYou have th=
ese magical experiences.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Davies said he was ready to move on from T=
hree Arrows by the end of last summer. =E2=80=9CI really spent so much time=
 meditating in Bali that I=E2=80=99m really just pretty zenned out,=E2=80=
=9D he said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yes right=C2=A0<em>he=C2=A0</em>is zenned out.=
 <em>He</em> played the crypto bubble perfectly.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sometimes a financial services firm will take =
too many bad risks, and it will go bust. And the normal way to go bust, for=
 a financial services firm, is that the failed firm will be=C2=A0<em>bought=
</em>, generally for a small amount of=C2=A0money, by some other, bigger fi=
rm that did not take as many risks. A big relatively sedate firm buys the f=
irm that flew a bit too close to the sun. But the failed firm doesn=E2=80=
=99t=C2=A0<em>disappear</em>; its assets and businesses and employees are s=
till there, and the sedate firm buys them, and then it decides what to do w=
ith them. And conceptually there are two approaches:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CThey took too much risk, whi=
ch led them to go bust. We are smarter than that, so we are going to ruthle=
ssly impose=C2=A0<em>our=C2=A0</em>safer and more sedate culture on them, a=
nd get rid of all the dumb risks that led them to go bust.=E2=80=9D</li>=09=
<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CWe always admired their risk-tak=
ing and were kind of bored with ourselves, and now we were able to buy them=
 cheap. Oh oh oh we are not going to take=C2=A0<em>as many </em>risks as th=
ey did =E2=80=94=C2=A0that blew them up after all =E2=80=94=C2=A0but surely=
 we can carefully graft=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</em>of their risk-taking cultur=
e onto our sedate culture in a way that will still be safe enough, but let =
us have more fun than we=E2=80=99ve been having.=E2=80=9D</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/31767466.257419/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Are stablecoins securities? A typical stableco=
in =E2=80=94=C2=A0Tether, Circle=E2=80=99s USDC, Binance=E2=80=99s BUSD, et=

<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You give $1 to a company; it gives yo=
u back a crypto token (the stablecoin).</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: =
5px;">The company presumably puts the $1 somewhere safe =E2=80=94=C2=A0a ba=
nk account, Treasury bills, something like that =E2=80=94=C2=A0though there=
 is certainly room for hijinks here, and your legal rights are not necessar=
ily=C2=A0<em>great</em>.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The compa=
ny promises to give you back the $1 on demand.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-b=
ottom: 5px;">The company presumably earns interest on its investments, whic=
h makes this a nice business, for it.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5p=
x;">The company mostly does=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>pay you interest on your=
 stablecoin.</li>
</ol>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Using prongs of the so-called Howey test=E2=80=
=94a court precedent that gives conditions under which something can be con=
sidered a security=E2=80=94the SEC alleged in its Binance complaint that BU=
SD buyers participated in a common enterprise with Binance, which did =E2=
=80=9Cearn returns through various forms of capital deployment.=E2=80=9D It=
 also said that Binance marketed BUSD=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cprofit-earning pot=
ential,=E2=80=9D including a reward program that the SEC said =E2=80=9Cprom=
ised interest payments to BUSD investors merely for holding BUSD.=E2=80=9D<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Notably, the SEC said an expectation of profit=
 for BUSD investors derived from these rewards, which it described as =E2=
=80=9Cthe potential for direct, interestlike payments made by Binance, in p=
art from the proceeds of deploying BUSD investors=E2=80=99 capital.=E2=80=
=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This may be a key argument for the SEC, since =
it must confront the contention that stablecoins aren=E2=80=99t expected by=
 buyers to appreciate in value but instead to stand-in for a dollar, euro o=
r other traditional currency and intended to be used for things like paymen=
ts or storing value.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My view is that a stablecoin is probably not a=
 security and the SEC is wrong about BUSD, but I don=E2=80=99t hold that vi=

.=E2=80=9D Look at the list of securities.=C2=A0Is a stablecoin =E2=80=9Cev=
idence of indebtedness,=E2=80=9D or a =E2=80=9Cnote=E2=80=9D? I mean, kind =

 is=C2=A0not quite the same sort of legally binding promise you=E2=80=99d f=

oin as debt then it wouldn=E2=80=99t work. And =E2=80=9Cevidence of indebte=

ity.=C2=A0</p>

That money is being used to fund about 50% of North Korea=E2=80=99s ballist=
ic missile program, U.S. officials say, which has been developed in tandem =
with its nuclear weapons.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Jeff Bezos, the world=E2=80=99s third-richest =

azon.com Inc. for $114.77.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The share purchase, disclosed in a filing, is =
his first in records going back to 2002. The Amazon founder is better known=
 for selling =E2=80=94 he=E2=80=99s unloaded roughly $30 billion worth of s=
tock since 2002 to fund his various pursuits, from rocket company Blue Orig=
in to personal pleasures like his new $500 million superyacht Koru. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The May 25 stock purchase has made him about $=
10 richer, as Amazon shares traded around $124 on Friday, boosted by a broa=
der tech rally that pushed the S&P 500 Index into a bull market this we=
ek.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bezos=E2=80=99 move has confounded Amazon anal=
ysts and stock watchers who don=E2=80=99t know what to make of the singular=
 purchase. Social media was abuzz with theories, with one person speculatin=
g that Bezos planned to give the physical certificate as a gift and others =
quipping that he must have accidentally clicked the buy button on his broke=
rage account. Amazon and a representative for Bezos declined to comment on =
the purchase.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If I were Jeff Bezos I=C2=A0would absolutely o=
pen a Robinhood account and buy one share of Amazon and then email my discl=
osure lawyers the confirmation saying =E2=80=9Clol sorry.=E2=80=9D If <em>I=
=C2=A0</em>bought one share of Amazon, nobody would write articles about it=
. But when Jeff Bezos did, people did. Is that not reason enough? Two big p=
roblems in modern economics are turning attention into money, and turning m=
oney into attention; buying one share of Amazon is a pretty efficient way t=
o do the latter.</p>
